text.title: GraphQL Operations: DataLoader, Errors, and Security
text.summary: DataLoader N+1 prevention, error envelope format, depth/complexity limits, and production security rules.

rule.statement: Every resolver that loads a related entity must use DataLoader. DataLoader instances are scoped per-request in GraphQL context; never module-global.
rule.rationale: A module-global DataLoader leaks data across requests. Without DataLoader, a list of 100 orders fires 100 separate user queries — silent quadratic behavior in prod.

example.note: Module-global loader caches data from one request and serves it to another user's request.

rule.statement: Return errors in the GraphQL errors array with extensions.code as a stable enum string. Clients must never parse the message field for logic.
rule.rationale: message strings change with every release; a stable code enum allows clients to handle specific errors without coupling to prose.

rule.statement: Apply depth limiting (max 8–10) and query complexity limits (max 100–500) on every public GraphQL endpoint.
rule.rationale: Without limits, a single deeply nested or complex query can DoS the server or drain the database connection pool.

rule.statement: Use persisted queries in production: clients send a hash, server resolves to vetted operations. Disable introspection for non-public schemas or gate behind auth.
rule.rationale: Persisted queries close injection and DoS surface in one move. Introspection leaks the full schema to unauthenticated attackers.

text.reference: TypeScript DataLoader batch function with key ordering
